request.question: Which English actor, born in 1957 and considered a method actor, starred in the 2009 musical drama film "Nine."

title: Aaron Sidwell
Alexander Fiske-Harrison
Hugo Johnstone-Burt
Daniel Day-Lewis
Michael Ironside
Christian Bale filmography
Falling Awake (film)
Rhythm (2000 film)
Nine (2009 live-action film)
Dilip Kumar
sentences: Aaron Michael Sidwell (born 12 September 1988) is an English actor and singer .

He is best known for playing villain Steven Beale in the British soap opera EastEnders, originating the role of Michael Dork in Loserville in the West End and fronting his own pop group, The Boston Plan.
He was also the first British actor to play Johnny in Green Day's American Idiot musical.
Sidwell is a method actor, known for using Stanislavski technique
Alexander Rupert Fiske-Harrison (born 22 July 1976) is an English prize-winning author and journalist, broadcaster and conservationist.
His writing has been noted for his immersion in his subject matter.
He also trained and worked for some years as a Method actor.
Hugo Johnstone-Burt (born 1987/1988) is a Scottish-born Australian actor.
He grew up in Sydney and decided to become an actor after attending his first drama class.
Johnstone-Burt graduated from the National Institute of Dramatic Art in 2009.
He appeared in small roles in Australian dramas "Rake", "Sea Patrol" and "", before he was cast as Fish Lamb in "Cloudstreet".
The role earned him two ASTRA Award nominations.
Johnstone-Burt went on to star in "Carelesss Love" and he took on the role of Constable Hugh Collins in ABC1's "Miss Fisher's Murder Mysteries".
In 2012, Johnstone-Burt appeared in "Tricky Business" and filmed a guest role in "Home and Away".
He has also appeared in the musical drama film "Goddess" (2013) and the disaster film "San Andreas" (2015).
Sir Daniel Michael Blake Day-Lewis (born 29 April 1957) is an English actor who holds both British and Irish citizenship.
Born and raised in London, he excelled on stage at the National Youth Theatre, before being accepted at the Bristol Old Vic Theatre School, which he attended for three years.
Despite his traditional actor training at the Bristol Old Vic, he is considered to be a method actor, known for his constant devotion to and research of his roles.
He would often remain completely in character for the duration of the shooting schedules of his films, even to the point of adversely affecting his health.
He is one of the most selective actors in the film industry, having starred in only five films since 1998, with as many as five years between roles.
Protective of his private life, he rarely gives interviews and makes very few public appearances.
Frederick Reginald Ironside (born February 12, 1950) is a Canadian actor best known by his stage name Michael Ironside.
He has worked as a voice actor, producer, film director, and screenwriter in movie and television series in various Canadian and American productions.
He is best known for playing villains and "tough guy" heroes, though he has also portrayed sympathetic characters.
Ironside is a method actor, who stays in character between filming scenes.
British actor Christian Bale has starred in various films, as well as advertisements and a video game.
He made his acting debut in 1986, on the television film "".
The following year, he made his film debut starring alongside John Malkovich and Miranda Richardson in the war film "Empire of the Sun".
Bale's role of a young boy, interned in China by the Japanese, received praise from most film critics.
Two years later, Bale had a minor role in "Henry V", a drama film based on William Shakespeare's play "The Life of Henry the Fifth".
It has been considered one of the best Shakespeare film adaptations ever made.
In 1992, Bale starred as Jack Kelly in the Walt Disney musical drama "Newsies", which was a critical and commercial failure; however, it gained a cult following.
He received a role in the 1994 drama "Little Women", which garnered positive reviews.
Bale lent his voice for the Disney animated film "Pocahontas" in 1995, although it received a mixed reception and attained box office success.
He starred as British journalist Arthur Stuart in the Todd Haynes-directed drama "Velvet Goldmine" (1998).
Although critics were divided on the film, Bale's role was "eagerly anticipated".
Bale portrayed Demetrius in the critically praised 1999 film "A Midsummer Night's Dream", an adaptation of Shakespeare's play of the same name, directed by Michael Hoffman.
The same year, he portrayed Jesus of Nazareth in the television movie "Mary, Mother of Jesus".
Falling Awake (originally titled Shine On) is a 2009 musical drama film directed by Agustín, who co-wrote it with Michael Baez and Doug Klozzner.
Andrew Cisneros stars as a Latino musician in New York City who attempts to overcome the problems if his poor neighborhood.
It premiered at the New York International Latino Film Festival and was released in the US on January 29, 2010.
Rhythm (Tamil: ரிதம் ) is a 2000 Tamil musical drama film written and directed by Vasanth and produced by V. Natarajan.
The film stars Arjun, Jyothika, Ramesh Aravind and Meena in lead roles with Lakshmi, Nagesh and Manivannan in supporting ones.
The music is composed by A. R. Rahman, while cinematography was predominantly handled by P. S. Vinod and Sreekar Prasad edited the film.
The film was released in September 2000, receiving highly positive reviews from critics and became commercially successful.
The film's soundtrack by A. R. Rahman was also very successful and received a cult for musical drama films .
Nine is a 2009 romantic musical drama film directed and produced by Rob Marshall and written by Michael Tolkin and Anthony Minghella.
The film is an adaptation of the 1982 musical of the same name, which in turn is based on Federico Fellini's semi-autobiographical 1963 film "8½".
In addition to songs from the stage musical, all written by Maury Yeston, the film has three original songs, also written by Yeston.
The ensemble principal cast consists of Daniel Day-Lewis, Marion Cotillard, Penélope Cruz, Judi Dench, Fergie, Kate Hudson, Nicole Kidman, and Sophia Loren.
Dilip Kumar (born Muhammad Yusuf Khan; 11 December 1922) is an Indian film actor, producer and activist.
Also known as the "Tragedy King", he is credited with bringing realism to film acting since his first film which was released in 1944.
He is considered one of the greatest and most influential actors of all time.
He has been described as "the ultimate method actor" (natural actor) by Satyajit Ray.
Kumar is one of the biggest Indian movie stars, and a pioneer of method acting, predating Hollywood method actors such as Marlon Brando.
Kumar debuted as an actor in the film "Jwar Bhata" (1944), produced by Bombay Talkies.
His career has spanned over six decades and over 65 films.

Kumar is known for roles in films such as the romantic "Andaz" (1949), the heartwarming "Babul" (1950), the impassioned "Deedar" (1951), the swashbuckling "Aan" (1952), the dramatic "Devdas" (1955), the comical "Azaad" (1955), "Naya Daur" (1957), "Yahudi" (1958), "Madhumati" (1958), "Kohinoor" (1960), the epic historical "Mughal-e-Azam" (1960), the social "Gunga Jamuna" (1961) and "Ram Aur Shyam" (1967).

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

hop1_docs: Dilip Kumar | Dilip Kumar (born Muhammad Yusuf Khan; 11 December 1922) is an Indian film actor, producer and activist.  Also known as the "Tragedy King", he is credited with bringing realism to film acting since his first film which was released in 1944.  He is considered one of the greatest and most influential actors of all time.  He has been described as "the ultimate method actor" (natural actor) by Satyajit Ray.  Kumar is one of the biggest Indian movie stars, and a pioneer of method acting, predating Hollywood method actors such as Marlon Brando.  Kumar debuted as an actor in the film "Jwar Bhata" (1944), produced by Bombay Talkies.  His career has spanned over six decades and over 65 films.  Kumar is known for roles in films such as the romantic "Andaz" (1949), the heartwarming "Babul" (1950), the impassioned "Deedar" (1951), the swashbuckling "Aan" (1952), the dramatic "Devdas" (1955), the comical "Azaad" (1955), "Naya Daur" (1957), "Yahudi" (1958), "Madhumati" (1958), "Kohinoor" (1960), the epic historical "Mughal-e-Azam" (1960), the social "Gunga Jamuna" (1961) and "Ram Aur Shyam" (1967).

Daniel Day-Lewis | Sir Daniel Michael Blake Day-Lewis (born 29 April 1957) is an English actor who holds both British and Irish citizenship.  Born and raised in London, he excelled on stage at the National Youth Theatre, before being accepted at the Bristol Old Vic Theatre School, which he attended for three years.  Despite his traditional actor training at the Bristol Old Vic, he is considered to be a method actor, known for his constant devotion to and research of his roles.  He would often remain completely in character for the duration of the shooting schedules of his films, even to the point of adversely affecting his health.  He is one of the most selective actors in the film industry, having starred in only five films since 1998, with as many as five years between roles.  Protective of his private life, he rarely gives interviews and makes very few public appearances.

Jim Sturgess | James Anthony Sturgess (born 16 May 1981) is an English actor and singer-songwriter.  His breakthrough role was appearing as Jude in the musical romance drama film "Across the Universe" (2007).  In 2008, he played the male lead role of Ben Campbell in "21".  In 2009, he played Gavin Kossef in the crime drama "Crossing Over", appearing with Harrison Ford, Ray Liotta and Ashley Judd.  In 2010, Sturgess starred in the film, "The Way Back", directed by Peter Weir.  Sturgess co-starred in the epic science fiction film "Cloud Atlas", which began filming in September 2011 and was released in October 2012.

Mike Norris (actor) | Michael R. "Mike" Norris (born October 4, 1962) is an American actor who has acted in over two dozen films.  He is the son of the more famous actor and martial arts champion Chuck Norris.  He starred in the 1986 drama film "Born American" and the 1991 action film "".  He also directed and starred in the 2009 Christian film, "Birdie & Bogey".

Michael Fassbender | Michael Fassbender (born 2 April 1977) is an Irish actor.  His feature film debut was in the fantasy war epic "300" (2007) as a Spartan warrior; his earlier roles included various stage productions, as well as starring roles on television such as in the HBO miniseries "Band of Brothers" (2001) and the Sky One fantasy drama "Hex" (2004–05).  He first came to prominence for his role as IRA activist Bobby Sands in "Hunger" (2008), for which he won a British Independent Film Award.  Subsequent roles include in the independent film "Fish Tank" (2009), as a Royal Marines lieutenant in "Inglourious Basterds" (2009), as Edward Rochester in the 2011 film adaptation of "Jane Eyre", as Carl Jung in "A Dangerous Method" (2011), as the sentient android David 8 in "Prometheus" (2012) and its sequel, "" (2017), and in the musical comedy-drama "Frank" (2014) as an eccentric musician loosely inspired by Frank Sidebottom.

ARY Film Award for Best Actor | The ARY Film Award for Best Actor is one of the ARY Film Awards of Merit presented annually by the ARY Digital Network and Entertainment Channel to male actor working in the film industry.  Best Actor is considered to be one of five most important awards of the ARY Film Awards, as it represents the main icon of cinematic industry on which all aspects of film such as directing, acting, music composing, writing, editing and other efforts that put forth into a drama is depending.  This award is one of the two Best Actor awards in ceremony, in which one is awarded to relevant film actor only by the decision of ceremony Jury, while other is being awarded on Viewers Voting's.

To-Day | To-Day is a 1917 silent film drama directed by Ralph Ince and starring Florence Reed.  A story about prostitution, this film is based on a 1913 stage play,"Today", by George Broadhurst and Abraham S. Schomer and starred Emily Stevens which ran for an astounding 280 performances in eight months time.  Actors Gus Weinburg and Alice Gale are the only actors in the film that appeared in the play.  It is considered to be a lost film.
hop1_titles: ARY Film Award for Best Actor
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: English actor born 1957 who is a method actor.

- Entity: Daniel Day-Lewis — supported facts: "Sir Daniel Michael Blake Day-Lewis (born 29 April 1957)"; described as an "English actor"; "he is considered to be a method actor".
- No other passage names an English actor born in 1957 or described as a method actor; no direct mention of the film "Nine" in any passage.
- Missing fact (blocks answer): confirmation that this actor "starred in the 2009 musical drama film 'Nine'." Bridge entity: Daniel Day-Lewis → his filmography / role in Nine (cast credit).
- Next-hop retrieval (priority order):
  1. "Nine (2009 film)" — fetch the lede and "Cast" section on the film's page.
  2. "Daniel Day-Lewis" — fetch the lede/infobox and "Filmography" or "Career" sections.
  3. Search: "Daniel Day-Lewis Nine 2009 cast" (or "Nine 2009 cast list").
  4. If needed, "Nine (2009) cast" on IMDb or a reliable filmography source.
hop2_docs: Nine (2009 live-action film) | Nine is a 2009 romantic musical drama film directed and produced by Rob Marshall and written by Michael Tolkin and Anthony Minghella.  The film is an adaptation of the 1982 musical of the same name, which in turn is based on Federico Fellini's semi-autobiographical 1963 film "8½".  In addition to songs from the stage musical, all written by Maury Yeston, the film has three original songs, also written by Yeston.  The ensemble principal cast consists of Daniel Day-Lewis, Marion Cotillard, Penélope Cruz, Judi Dench, Fergie, Kate Hudson, Nicole Kidman, and Sophia Loren.
List of awards and nominations received by Daniel Day-Lewis | List of awards and nominations received by Daniel Day-Lewis

Tamasin Day-Lewis | Lydia Tamasin Day-Lewis (born 17 September 1953) is an English television chef and food critic.  Day-Lewis is the daughter of the poet Cecil Day-Lewis and actress Jill Balcon and the sister of the actor Daniel Day-Lewis.

Cecil Day-Lewis | Cecil Day-Lewis (or Day Lewis) (27 April 1904 – 22 May 1972), often writing as C. Day-Lewis, was an Anglo-Irish poet and the Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom from 1968 until his death in 1972.  He also wrote mystery stories under the pseudonym of Nicholas Blake.  He was the father of actor Daniel Day-Lewis and documentary filmmaker and television chef Tamasin Day-Lewis.

The Boxer (1997 film) | The Boxer is a 1997 sports-drama film by Irish director Jim Sheridan.  Starring Daniel Day-Lewis and Emily Watson, the film centers on the life of a boxer and former Provisional IRA volunteer Danny Flynn, played by Day-Lewis, who is trying to "go straight" after his release from prison.  The film is the third collaboration between Sheridan and Day-Lewis, and portrays the increase of splinter groups within the IRA.

Phantom Thread | Phantom Thread is an upcoming American drama film, set in London's fashion world in the 1950s, shot, written and directed by Paul Thomas Anderson.  The film stars Daniel Day-Lewis, Lesley Manville, Richard Graham, Fraser Delaney and Vicky Krieps.  It is reportedly Daniel Day-Lewis's final performance in a film, following four decades in the profession.  The music will be composed by longtime Anderson collaborator and Radiohead member Jonny Greenwood.  The film is set to be released on December 25, 2017.
Bill Lewis III | Bill Lewis III is a fictional character on the CBS daytime soap opera "Guiding Light".  He has been portrayed by Daniel Cosgrove from 2002 to 2005 and 2007 to 2009.
hop2_titles: List of awards and nominations received by Daniel Day-Lewis
response.summary_2: Daniel Day-Lewis starred in the 2009 musical drama film Nine. Daniel Day-Lewis was born 29 April 1957 and is considered to be a method actor.
